what_it_does: This file declares a check named prose_clean and provides a check(value, **params) function that dynamically imports prose_check from /home/tim/company/design/_system, calls check_prose(value), and returns a standardized pass/fail result with human-readable reasons.
observations: Module-level CHECK dict holds id, label, and description metadata.
The docstring frames the check as a deterministic operator-prose leakage floor (G3·S3a) and notes the underlying judgment was moved off the model after two failed prompt calibrations.
check() mutates sys.path by inserting an absolute local directory before importing prose_check.
The import of prose_check happens at runtime inside the check function, not at module top level.
Returned shape is {"passed": bool, "reasons": list of "kind: match" strings}.
standouts: Hardcoded absolute filesystem path /home/tim/company/design/_system for the dependency.
Runtime sys.path mutation on every check invocation.
Explicit design rationale in the docstring about moving voice judgment off the model.
conventions: Follows a check-module convention with a CHECK metadata dictionary and a check(value, **params) entry point.
Returns a normalized result object with passed and reasons keys.
Breaks typical import convention by doing a local, path-mutating dynamic import inside the function.
concerns: Hardcoded absolute path makes the file environment-dependent and non-portable.
Repeated sys.path.insert can accumulate duplicate entries across multiple calls.
No error handling if prose_check or check_prose is missing or raises.
Runtime import obscures the dependency from static analysis and import-time failures.
notes: A thin, metadata-tagged wrapper that delegates deterministic prose-leakage detection to a sibling design-system module, but ties itself to a specific absolute path.
questions: Is /home/tim/company/design/_system guaranteed to exist in all deployment environments?
Why is the prose_check import performed inside check() rather than at module top level?
What schema does the value argument expect?
What are the possible values of hit kind and match returned by check_prose?
What do G3·S3a and cascade-referenceable mean in this codebase?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches actual behavior: it is a declared, deterministic wrapper that calls an underlying prose checker and returns pass/fail reasons. The only mismatch is the hardcoded absolute path, which is an implementation detail not mentioned in the purpose.
apparent_themes: Separation of voice/policy judgment from the model
intention_signals: The file seems to be trying to make the operator-prose leakage floor a stable, named, referenceable check while ensuring the actual judgment logic lives in a deterministic code module rather than a prompt.
novelty: The docstring explicitly documents the historical reason for moving the check off the model, and the implementation uses a runtime sys.path injection to reach a sibling design/_system module.
contribution: It contributes a named check entry point with metadata and a standardized result shape that connects the checks namespace to the underlying prose_check implementation.
summary_for_embedding: A check module that wraps a deterministic operator-prose leakage detector by dynamically importing prose_check from a hardcoded design/_system path and returning a pass/fail result with reasons.
intention_for_embedding: To expose the operator-prose leakage floor as a declared, cascade-referenceable check that delegates deterministic voice judgment to code instead of the model.

symbols.does: Function that dynamically imports prose_check from a hardcoded path, calls check_prose(value), and returns a standardized pass/fail result with reasons.
symbols.description: The check(value, **params) function is the runtime entry point for the prose_clean check. On each call it inserts /home/tim/company/design/_system into sys.path, imports prose_check, invokes check_prose(value), and translates the returned dictionary into a normalized shape containing a boolean passed flag and a list of reason strings formatted as "kind: match".
